title: Welsh rarebit
subtitle: Cheese, mustard, Worcestershire, ale, grilled on toast.
excerpt: A roux-thickened cheese sauce spiked with English mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and a splash of ale, spread thickly on toast and grilled until it puffs and blisters. More than cheese on toast — it is its own dish.
sourceNotes: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), public domain (early Welsh rabbit recipe using cheese melted with ale and mustard). Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — Welsh rarebit in the savouries chapter. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), the roux-thickened version with English mustard.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cheese sauce keeps in the fridge for up to three days. Bring to room temperature, spread on toast, and grill from cool — the sauce just takes another minute or two to puff.

content.text: Welsh rarebit is not simply cheese on toast: the cheese is made into a sauce, thickened to a spreading consistency, and grilled so it puffs and bronzes under direct heat. The result has a different texture from melted cheese — soft in the centre, blistered at the peaks, with a slight crust where the sauce meets the toast edge. The mustard and Worcestershire run through every mouthful.

Use a mature or extra-mature cheddar. A mild cheese melts smoothly but lacks the sharpness that defines the dish. Mature Red Leicester or a mix of half cheddar and half Red Leicester gives a more orange colour and a slightly sweeter flavour — a reasonable variation. Anything crumblier than mature cheddar tends to go grainy in the sauce.

items.prepNote: pale ale or stout; milk works if no beer is to hand

content.text: A flat whisk or wooden spoon keeps the sauce smooth. The grill needs to be at full heat when the rarebit goes under — the few minutes under a high grill are what create the characteristic puffed, bronzed surface.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter in a small saucepan over low-medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for one minute. Pour in {{beer}} of pale ale and stir briskly to combine. The sauce will thicken quickly into a paste. Stir over medium heat for another minute until it pulls away from the sides of the pan cleanly.

Remove from the heat. Add {{cheddar}} of grated mature cheddar in three batches, stirring between each addition until fully melted. Add {{english-mustard}} of English mustard, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, and {{white-pepper}} of white pepper. Stir until smooth and glossy. The mixture should hold its shape on a spoon and spread without running.

Heat the grill to its highest setting. Toast {{bread}} of bread slices on both sides. Place the toast on a grill tray. Spread the cheese mixture thickly over each slice, right to the edges, in a layer about 1 cm thick. A thin layer dries out under the grill; a thick layer puffs properly.

Grill for 3 to 5 minutes close to the heat element until the surface puffs up, bronzes in patches, and blisters at the edges. Watch it: the change from perfectly done to overdone takes less than a minute. Serve straight from the grill — the sauce firms up as it cools and loses that molten centre quality.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: The sauce and the grill are the two variables that most often go wrong.

items.cause: Cheese added to a sauce that was still on high heat, or the flour not fully cooked before cheese was added.
items.fix: Take the pan off the heat before adding cheese. Add in batches and stir each fully before adding the next.

items.symptom: Surface pale and rubbery after grilling
items.cause: Grill not hot enough, or too far from the heat element.
items.fix: Preheat the grill fully on maximum. Position the tray so the top of the rarebit is about 8 cm from the element.

items.cause: Spread on untoasted bread, or the sauce too thin.
items.fix: Toast both sides of the bread first so it can support the weight. The sauce should be thick enough to mound slightly.

items.cause: A very hoppy or bitter ale amplified by the cheese and Worcestershire.
items.fix: Use a milder ale, or substitute half the ale with whole milk for a gentler result.

content.text: Welsh rarebit has appeared in British cookery books since at least 1725, when it turns up in cookery manuscripts as "Welsh rabbit" — a humorous name for a dish that mocked the Welsh for serving cheese where meat would be expected. Hannah Glasse's 1747 recipe is close to what is made today. It was a savoury traditionally served at the end of a formal dinner, as a restorative after the claret, or as a late supper in its own right. Today it is primarily a lunch dish, served with a watercress salad and a pot of tea, or as a late-night thing after a cold evening out.
